text.title: Python Type Hints Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules covering union syntax, boundary annotation, ignore-comment policy, structural typing, payload-vs-body type choice, and incremental adoption.

rule.statement: Use PEP 604 unions (`X | None`, `int | str`) and built-in generics (`list[T]`, `dict[K, V]`) exclusively in new code. Ban `Optional`, `List`, `Dict`, `Tuple` imports from `typing` via ruff rule UP007 (`pyupgrade`).
rule.rationale: Mixing `Optional[X]` and `X | None` in one repo triggers linter warnings and degrades readability. PEP 604 syntax is shorter and Python 3.10+ runtime-supported. Pick one and enforce it project-wide via auto-fix.

rule.statement: Annotate boundary signatures first: HTTP handlers, queue consumers, DB models, library public exports. Internal helpers get arg + return annotations only; locals only when mypy complains. Type the boundary before the body.
rule.rationale: Boundaries are where bugs cross module ownership lines; that's where types pay off most. Annotating every local variable bloats diff without proportional bug-catch.

rule.statement: Never write `# type: ignore` without a specific error code. Use `# type: ignore[attr-defined]` / `[arg-type]` / `[return-value]`. Enable `warn_unused_ignores = true` so stale ignores fail CI.
rule.rationale: Bare ignores mask future bugs silently. Specific codes document the reason and allow CI to surface ignores that no longer apply once the upstream issue is fixed.

rule.statement: For cross-package contracts use `typing.Protocol`. Use `ABC` only when you control all implementers AND need shared concrete methods. Add `@runtime_checkable` only when actually calling `isinstance(x, Proto)` at runtime.
rule.rationale: Protocol avoids ABC inheritance coupling — third-party types satisfy a Protocol structurally without inheriting from your package. `runtime_checkable` is opt-in because it makes `isinstance` walk the MRO, which is slow on hot paths.

rule.statement: `TypedDict` for cache payloads, queue messages, and internal dicts crossing module boundaries. `Pydantic` (v2) for HTTP request/response bodies and external-input validation. Never define both `TypedDict` and `Pydantic` for the same data shape — pick one per shape and use everywhere.
rule.rationale: TypedDict is zero-runtime-cost typing for already-validated data. Pydantic adds validation + serialization but costs CPU on every instantiation. Maintaining parallel definitions for the same shape doubles the maintenance and the two drift apart.

rule.statement: Never run `mypy --strict` repo-wide on an untyped codebase. Adopt file-by-file: enable strict on touched files only via `mypy --strict $(git diff --name-only --diff-filter=AM main -- '*.py')`. Fix one error class per pass (no-untyped-def → var-annotated → arg-type).
rule.rationale: Strict on day 1 produces 1000+ errors and freezes adoption. Incremental gating lets the team make continuous progress while never regressing typed files.
